data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The Late Bronze Age collapse was primarily caused by a synchronized systems collapse triggered by the disruption of the 'palace economy' redistributive network due to a cascade of maritime trade route failures.", "mechanism": "Palace elites depended on long-distance maritime exchange of bulk goods (copper, tin, grain) to produce bronze and feed specialized labor. When a combination of piracy and climatic shifts (drought) made Aegean-Levantine sea routes unreliable, the input-output balance of the redistributive system broke. Without bronze to pay soldiers or grain to feed artisans, the interdependent palaces lost their coercive and economic grip simultaneously, leading to rapid administrative collapse and elite flight.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Assyria"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence shows uninterrupted high-volume trade in copper and tin throughout the crisis period.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "The palace archives record a gradual, multi-century decline in administrative complexity rather than a sudden cessation.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Egypt survived because the Nile's agricultural base was a domestic, non-trade-dependent redistributive system, allowing the state to weather the trade shock. This explains the simultaneity: the collapse was a network failure, not independent local events."}
